title: The Little Things

jokes.text: SERGEANT PEPPER, WHERE'S THE REST OF THE LONELY HEARTS CLUB BAND?
jokes.setup: A group of scruffy-looking teenage burnouts are gathered together in school
jokes.punchline: An authority figure compares them to the Beatles' Sgt. Pepper album cover — an insult disguised as a pop culture reference
jokes.explanation: A competent period-appropriate insult. Works as character establishment for both the freaks and the adult world's condescension toward them. Not particularly original but lands naturally.

jokes.text: YEAH, IF YOU'RE A REPUBLICAN. / OH, AND YOU'RE NOT? / NO, I'M A DEMOCRAT. / YOU KNOW, EVERYONE'S A DEMOCRAT TILL THEY GET A LITTLE MONEY. THEN THEY COME TO THEIR SENSES.
jokes.setup: The family is discussing Bush's visit; Lindsay reveals she's a Democrat
jokes.punchline: Harold's condescending wisdom — 'everyone's a Democrat till they get a little money' — delivered with complete paternal certainty
jokes.explanation: Harold's conservative certainty is a well-used character note. The line itself is a real thing people say, which makes it feel authentic rather than invented. Good Harold establishment.

jokes.text: THE PORN STAR? [in response to 'George Bush is coming']
jokes.setup: Someone announces that George Bush is coming to speak at the school
jokes.punchline: A burnout deadpans 'The porn star?' — confusing the Vice President with a then-famous adult film performer named Georgina Spelvin or simply being deliberately obtuse
jokes.explanation: Sharp economy — three words that land with surprise. The character's obliviousness (genuine or performed) is perfectly in voice for a burnout who doesn't track mainstream politics. The brief straight-man correction sells it.

jokes.text: BUSH PROBABLY DOESN'T KNOW ANYTHING ABOUT THE TUBA.
jokes.setup: Amy is nervous because the band has to play 'Hail to the Chief' and there's a lot of tuba
jokes.punchline: A friend reassures her with the deeply unhelpful and logically absurd observation that the VP doesn't know anything about tuba
jokes.explanation: A genuinely funny piece of absurdist logic. The joke works because it's delivered as if it's actual comfort, but the reasoning is completely nonsequitur — why would the VP's ignorance of tuba make Amy less nervous? The specificity of 'tuba' adds to the absurdity. Clean structure, good economy.

jokes.text: [Burnout mimics 'Hail to the Chief' badly out of tune while Amy reacts with horror]
jokes.setup: Amy is stressed about playing 'Hail to the Chief' correctly; a friend is trying to be reassuring
jokes.punchline: The friend immediately undermines any reassurance by mimicking the song terribly, making Amy's situation feel worse — Amy's 'STOP!' sells it
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy — the irony of offering comfort then immediately doing the opposite thing is a reliable comedic structure. Works in the moment but not memorable.

jokes.text: HOW WE EVER GONNA PLAN OUR COUP? / DON'T EVEN JOKE, DESARIO. I CAN GET YOU THROWN IN JAIL JUST FOR SAYING THAT.
jokes.setup: The freaks are being cleared out by Secret Service; someone complains about the restrictions
jokes.punchline: Daniel sarcastically asks how they'll plan their coup; the agent takes it completely seriously and threatens jail — the paranoia of Secret Service overreaction to obvious teenage sarcasm
jokes.explanation: Good comedic interplay between teenage sarcasm and authority overreaction. The agent's completely humorless response is a solid straight-man beat.

jokes.text: COOOOOUP. COUP. COUP. [Daniel chanting softly as he's escorted away]
jokes.setup: The security guard has just threatened Daniel with jail for joking about a coup
jokes.punchline: Rather than backing down, Daniel quietly keeps saying 'coup' under his breath as he walks away — defiant but tiny
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character comedy. The escalation from the previous joke to Daniel quietly continuing to say 'coup' is perfectly in character — the low-grade teenage defiance that's too small to punish but too persistent to ignore. The repetition and the soft delivery make it funnier than the original setup. Strong character integration: only Daniel would do this.

jokes.text: ONCE YOU START DOWN THAT DARK PATH, FOREVER WILL IT DOMINATE YOUR DESTINY. AT LEAST THAT'S WHAT YODA ALWAYS SAYS.
jokes.setup: A geek is dispensing wisdom about some social situation
jokes.punchline: The wisdom turns out to be a Yoda quote, which the geek attributes to Yoda by name as if citing an authority — and the qualifier 'at least that's what Yoda always says' makes it self-aware and funny
jokes.explanation: Solid geek character moment — applying Yoda as a genuine life authority is perfectly in voice. The self-aware addition of 'at least that's what Yoda always says' elevates it above a straight reference drop.

jokes.text: NOT ALL OUR CHEERLEADERS ARE PRETTY. / YOU CAN SAY THAT AGAIN. / TODD, SHUT UP. / I WASN'T TALKING ABOUT YOU. / GOD, WOULD YOU GET OVER YOURSELF?
jokes.setup: A group at Cindy's table is criticizing another school's cheerleaders for being unattractive; Cindy concedes not all their cheerleaders are pretty either
jokes.punchline: Todd immediately agrees, a cheerleader assumes he means her, he denies it, and she tells him to get over himself — a rapid-fire social disaster
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed social comedy — the rapid escalation from casual comment to social landmine to indignant denial to counter-accusation of vanity is efficiently executed. The cheerleader's 'would you get over yourself' is the best line, flipping the humiliation back.

jokes.text: SAM, YOU'RE NOT GONNA DEFEND ME? / WHAT? HE SAID HE WASN'T TALKING ABOUT YOU. / SO YOU'RE JUST GONNA LET IT GO? / WOULD YOU STOP? / I LIKE SAM. / WHAT, DO YOU WANT TO SEE US FIGHT? / I'D LIKE TO SEE THAT.
jokes.setup: Cindy wants Sam to defend her honor over Todd's comment; Sam tries to defuse logically
jokes.punchline: Sam's completely rational response ('he said he wasn't talking about you') is the wrong move; when he asks if she wants them to fight, Todd cheerfully says he'd like to see that — puncturing the macho posturing
jokes.explanation: Good character-driven awkwardness. Sam's total failure to understand that Cindy wants performance, not logic, is painfully real. Todd's 'I'd like to see that' is the unexpected button that gets the laugh.

jokes.text: MOUSETRAP! I WIN! / CONGRATULATIONS, BILL. MAYBE YOU CAN GET THE SCHOOL TO START A TEAM.
jokes.setup: Bill wins at Mousetrap (a children's board game) and celebrates enthusiastically
jokes.punchline: Neal sarcastically suggests the school start a Mousetrap team — pointing out the absurdity of being proud of winning a children's game
jokes.explanation: Functional geek comedy. Bill winning Mousetrap and being genuinely proud is perfectly in character. Neal's sarcastic response is delivered with his characteristic dry wit.

jokes.text: ALL SHE EVER WANTS TO DO IS MAKE OUT AND STUFF. / I'D KILL TO BE THAT BORED.
jokes.setup: Sam is complaining that his relationship with Cindy is boring because all she wants to do is make out
jokes.punchline: Bill immediately responds 'I'd kill to be that bored' — the inversion of Sam's complaint from a teenager who can't get a girlfriend
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. It's perfectly constructed: Sam delivers his 'problem' earnestly, and Bill's response completely reframes it without any words being wasted. 'I'd kill to be that bored' is highly quotable, perfectly in character for Bill (socially isolated geek), and lands with an immediacy that shows genuine comic craftsmanship. The economy is exceptional — six words that do the entire job.

jokes.text: WHY ARE YOU GOING OUT WITH HER? / BECAUSE SHE'S A GODDESS! / AM I THE LAST SANE MAN ON THIS GODFORSAKEN PLANET?
jokes.setup: Bill asks logically why Sam is going out with Cindy if he doesn't enjoy her company
jokes.punchline: Neal erupts with 'BECAUSE SHE'S A GODDESS!' then delivers the exasperated 'Am I the last sane man...' — Neal's grandiose self-regard makes even his exasperation funny
jokes.explanation: Neal's dramatic interruption is perfectly timed and character-specific — his grandiosity is what makes 'AM I THE LAST SANE MAN ON THIS GODFORSAKEN PLANET?' so funny. He is clearly not the sane man. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: PASS HER OVER HERE. I'D MOVE TO THE MALL IF SHE WANTED ME TO.
jokes.setup: Sam has been complaining that Cindy dragged him to the mall multiple times on their dates
jokes.punchline: Neal, who desperately wants a girlfriend like Cindy, says he'd move to the mall for her — turning Sam's complaint into a flex about how much more he'd appreciate her
jokes.explanation: A solid Neal moment — his desperation for female attention is well-established and this is a good expression of it. Not his funniest line but consistent character work.

jokes.text: UM, 'CAUSE I HATE IT THERE? / HOW COME EVERYONE ALWAYS THINKS I'M JOKING ALL THE TIME? SOMETHING TO DO WITH MY VOICE, ISN'T IT?
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks Ken why they never hang out at his house
jokes.punchline: Ken says 'cause I hate it there' so flatly that Lindsay laughs; then he adds a self-aware observation that his voice/delivery causes people to misread his sincerity — which is itself delivered in the exact same deadpan that causes the problem
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely excellent character comedy. The meta-joke is perfectly constructed: Ken's deadpan is so extreme that people can't tell when he's serious. His observation about this problem is DELIVERED IN THE SAME DEADPAN, which means his complaint about being misread is itself being misread — it's recursive. The line 'something to do with my voice, isn't it? That's what I think' has the exact cadence of Ken's character. Only Ken Miller could say this.

jokes.text: SHE WAS THE BEST MOMMY MONEY COULD BUY.
jokes.setup: Ken is opening up about being raised by a nanny named Catherine because his parents were too focused on their careers
jokes.punchline: His description of Catherine as 'the best mommy money could buy' — delivered with Ken's signature flat affect — is simultaneously funny and heartbreaking
jokes.explanation: Ken's line is a masterclass in Freaks and Geeks' tonal specialty — jokes that are funny and sad simultaneously. 'The best mommy money could buy' is bitter, self-aware, and perfectly economical. The phrase works because it applies a commercial metric to emotional need. Strong character integration — Ken's defensive irony as emotional armor is core to who he is.

jokes.text: IT'S, UH, IF I WAS DATING YOU WHEN YOU WERE JUST BORN, THINGS, UH, MIGHT BE A LITTLE DIFFERENT... BUT NOW YOU'RE ALL GIRL NOW, SO IT'S OK.
jokes.setup: Amy has just revealed she was born intersex and the doctors surgically made her female; Ken is struggling to respond
jokes.punchline: Ken's attempt to be accepting lands in the most awkward place possible — he essentially says 'if you were a newborn with ambiguous genitalia I might have had a problem dating you, but since you're all girl now, we're good'
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely daring comedy writing. Ken's response is perfectly calibrated: his heart is in the right place (he's trying to be accepting) but his verbal execution is spectacularly bad. The logic he deploys — comparing a dating prospect to a newborn intersex baby — is absurd yet somehow follows from his deadpan literalism. This is a moment only Ken could deliver.

jokes.text: THANKS, KEN. / YEAH. YOU KNOW, IT'S, UH, I, UH, I HAD MY APPENDIX OUT, SO, YOU KNOW, I'VE BEEN THERE.
jokes.setup: Amy thanks Ken for his acceptance of her revelation about being born intersex
jokes.punchline: Ken, reaching for a way to establish solidarity, compares her experience of having surgery to his appendectomy — the most tonally inappropriate comparison possible
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. It's a perfect comedic catastrophe: Ken is genuinely trying to empathize, reaches for a shared surgical experience, and lands on 'I had my appendix out, so I've been there' — as if that's remotely equivalent. The absurdity is stunning, the character integration is flawless (Ken's emotional incompetence expressed through misguided literalism), and Amy's laugh tells us it works. It's funny because it's KIND, just spectacularly miscalibrated. This is exactly the kind of joke that defines a character.

jokes.text: I DON'T WANT TO DO THAT. / WHY? / BECAUSE I'M A DEMOCRAT.
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso excitedly tells Lindsay he's arranged for her to ask the first question to VP Bush
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's flat refusal and its entirely political rationale — 'because I'm a Democrat' — delivered with teenage certainty
jokes.explanation: A clean character beat for Lindsay. Her principled obstinacy is both funny and recognizable as a teenager who's just discovered political identity.

jokes.text: I GUESS ME AND MY HIPPIE FRIENDS WERE ALL JUST WASTING OUR TIME AT BERKELEY DEMONSTRATING AND STOPPING AN UNJUST WAR. PROBABLY SHOULDN'T HAVE BOTHERED.
jokes.setup: Lindsay refuses to ask Bush a question; Rosso tries to guilt-trip her with his activist past
jokes.punchline: Rosso's self-righteous callback to Berkeley — 'probably shouldn't have bothered' — is the passive-aggressive guilt-trip of a man who can't just make his point without weaponizing his own sacrifice
jokes.explanation: Good Rosso character work. His tendency to use his activist past as emotional leverage is a consistent and funny character trait. The 'probably shouldn't have bothered' button is the right note of self-pitying sarcasm.

jokes.text: I'VE GOT THE BEST JOB IN THE WORLD! 12 GRAND A YEAR, AND I'M OVERPAID.
jokes.setup: Rosso has just successfully convinced Lindsay to ask the question and is elated
jokes.punchline: His joy is immediately deflated by his own self-aware assessment of his job's meager compensation — the joke is that he loves his meaningless, underpaid job
jokes.explanation: One of Rosso's best lines. The structure is perfect: genuine joy immediately undercut by self-deprecating reality. '12 grand a year and I'm overpaid' is quotable, specific, and the 'and I'm overpaid' twist makes it more than just complaining. This is Rosso's essence — earnest idealism coexisting with complete self-awareness about his station.

jokes.text: DID YOU HAVE A GOOD LUNCH? / I THOUGHT THE CAFETERIA FOOD LOOKED A LITTLE GROSS, SO I HAD AN APPLE. IT WAS ACTUALLY PRETTY GOOD. IF YOU LIKE SALISBURY STEAK.
jokes.setup: Sam and Cindy are walking together between classes; the conversation is painfully banal
jokes.punchline: Cindy describes having an apple as 'actually pretty good' then immediately undermines this with 'if you like Salisbury steak' — a non-sequitur that reveals how little they have to talk about
jokes.explanation: This is quietly excellent Feig-style comedy. The Salisbury steak non-sequitur is surreally meaningless — what does an apple have to do with Salisbury steak? — but it perfectly captures the desperateness of filling dead air between two incompatible people. The comedy IS the banality. Understated but very well observed.

jokes.text: I WANT TO ASK HIM ABOUT THE ALIENS AT ROSWELL.
jokes.setup: Lindsay and the freaks are brainstorming serious political questions for VP Bush; someone suggests Iran-Contra, someone suggests trickle-down economics
jokes.punchline: Kim immediately proposes asking about Roswell aliens — a complete tonal non-sequitur that reveals she has no interest in actual political discourse
jokes.explanation: Well-placed character beat for Kim — her complete disinterest in political nuance and preference for conspiracy theory is perfectly in voice. The escalation from Iran-Contra to trickle-down to Roswell is a well-structured comedic ladder.

jokes.text: [BAD ENGLISH ACCENT] IF IT WERE ME, I'D TAKE HER TO A BROADWAY SHOW. / BROADWAY? WE'RE IN MICHIGAN. / [NORMAL VOICE] DINNER THEATER?
jokes.setup: The geeks are brainstorming ideas for Sam's date with Cindy
jokes.punchline: Bill puts on a terrible English accent to suggest Broadway (inherently absurd), is reminded they're in Michigan, and immediately deflates to 'Dinner theater?' — the entire arc from aspiration to sad reality
jokes.explanation: The inexplicable English accent is what makes this work — Bill's commitment to a bit for no reason is perfectly weird. The rapid deflation to dinner theater captures the geeks' limited world very well.

jokes.text: THE JERK IS PLAYING AT THE DISCOUNT THEATER. THAT'S NOT EXACTLY ROMANTIC. / LAUGHTER IS THE ULTIMATE APHRODISIAC. GET A WOMAN LAUGHING AND YOU'VE GOT A WOMAN LOVING.
jokes.setup: The geeks debate romantic date ideas; someone mentions The Jerk is playing
jokes.punchline: Bill delivers 'laughter is the ultimate aphrodisiac' as if it's profound romantic wisdom — from a 14-year-old who has never dated anyone
jokes.explanation: Bill dispensing romantic wisdom is inherently funny given his total lack of romantic experience. 'Get a woman laughing and you've got a woman loving' is the kind of thing a 14-year-old thinks sounds wise. Good character comedy.

jokes.text: YOU COULD MAKE HER SOMETHING. LIKE OUT OF PAPER MACHE. / YEAH, THAT'S GREAT. IT'S FROM THE HEART.
jokes.setup: The geeks are suggesting romantic gifts Sam could give Cindy
jokes.punchline: Someone suggests paper mache, and this is immediately validated as 'from the heart' — the complete obliviousness to what a teenage girl would actually want
jokes.explanation: Solid geek comedy — their sincerity about bad ideas is consistently funny. Paper mache as a romantic gift is a great specific detail.

jokes.text: HEY, HARRIS? WHAT KIND OF PRESENTS DO YOU GET JUDITH? / JUDITH HAS VERY PARTICULAR TASTES. ABOUT THE ONLY THING SHE EVER WANTS IS SCENTED OILS AND PLENTY OF TIME WITH HER MAN. / Everybody: ECCH!
jokes.setup: The geeks consult Harris (an even geekier geek) about what girls like as gifts
jokes.punchline: Harris's answer — scented oils and time with her man — is simultaneously gross and reveals an unexpectedly active romantic life that horrifies everyone present
jokes.explanation: Harris is a scene-stealer. The joke works on multiple levels: (1) the absurdity of consulting him at all, (2) that he has a girlfriend when more socially competent geeks don't, (3) the specific grossness of 'scented oils and plenty of time with her man' suggesting actual intimacy from the least likely source, (4) the collective 'ECCH!' The group reaction is the button that makes it land.

jokes.text: YOUR GRANDMOTHER GAVE THIS TO ME WHEN I WAS JUST ABOUT CINDY'S AGE. IT'S AN HEIRLOOM. [...] AFTER ALL, THIS IS YOUR FIRST LOVE.
jokes.setup: Jean gives Sam a family heirloom necklace to give Cindy as a first-love gift
jokes.punchline: The elaborate sentimental weight placed on what turns out to be a relationship Sam is about to end — the audience knows what Jean doesn't
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is the main comedic engine here — the audience knows Sam is about to break up with Cindy, making Jean's ceremony around 'your first love' painful-funny. This gains more value on rewatch.

jokes.text: IT MEANS THAT SHE WAS PACKING BOTH A GUN AND THE HOLSTER.
jokes.setup: Ken is trying to explain Amy's intersex condition to the other freaks with appropriate delicacy
jokes.punchline: His chosen metaphor — 'packing both a gun and the holster' — is the least delicate possible framing, delivered by Ken with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: The metaphor is genuinely inventive — the gun/holster framing is characteristically Ken: trying to explain something with gruff pragmatism and landing somewhere completely inappropriate. Only Ken would reach for a weapons metaphor to describe anatomy. The specificity of the metaphor is what makes it land over a cruder alternative.

jokes.text: Rosso conducting a counseling session in a cramped temporary 'office' (a broom closet or similar) after being displaced by Secret Service
jokes.setup: Secret Service has taken over Rosso's actual office; he's been given a tiny substitute space
jokes.punchline: The visual of Rosso trying to maintain professional counselor gravitas while crammed into an absurdly small space
jokes.explanation: The running visual of Rosso's degraded circumstances (displaced by the VP's visit) is a structural comedy thread. The acknowledgment that it's 'kind of cramped' is understatement. Functions as setup for the counseling scene more than as a standalone gag.

jokes.text: I MIGHT BE GAY. / I SEE. THAT'S COOL. / AND, YOU KNOW, I THOUGHT YOU'D BE A GOOD PERSON TO TALK TO ABOUT IT, SINCE YOU'RE GAY. / I'M NOT GAY.
jokes.setup: Ken has gone to Mr. Rosso (who he believes is gay) to discuss his feelings about Amy
jokes.punchline: Ken's revelation that he came specifically because Rosso is gay is immediately undercut by Rosso saying he's not gay
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed two-part joke. First, Ken's confession of potentially being gay is played completely deadpan and Rosso's 'that's cool' is the perfect counselor response. Then the whole premise is destroyed when Ken reveals he chose Rosso because he assumed he was gay — and he's wrong. The structural beauty is that Ken came for support based on a false premise, and now both his problem AND his resource have collapsed simultaneously.

jokes.text: WHY DID YOU THINK THAT? / I DON'T KNOW. YOU JUST KINDA HAVE THIS WAY ABOUT YOU, AND I'VE NEVER SEEN YOU WITH A WOMAN. / I DON'T BRING DATES TO SCHOOL.
jokes.setup: Rosso asks Ken why he assumed he was gay
jokes.punchline: Ken's reasoning — 'you have this way about you' and 'I've never seen you with a woman' — is uncomfortably specific, and Rosso's flat 'I don't bring dates to school' is the perfect non-answer
jokes.explanation: Rosso's 'I don't bring dates to school' is the right note — technically irrefutable but doing nothing to actually resolve the ambiguity Ken raised. The comedy is in the non-resolution.

jokes.text: THERE'S NOTHING WRONG WITH BEING GAY. IT'S JUST NOT MY PERSONAL PREFERENCE. / I THINK I'D BETTER GET GOING. / KEN, YOU CAN STAY AND TALK ABOUT THIS. / NO, I DON'T THINK I CAN.
jokes.setup: After the revelation that Rosso isn't gay, the conversation has become deeply awkward for both of them
jokes.punchline: Rosso gamely tries to continue the counseling session; Ken's 'No, I don't think I can' is the most honest possible response to a situation that has completely imploded
jokes.explanation: Good cringe button on the Rosso scene. Ken's exit line is perfectly in character — he's not rude, just definitively done. 'No, I don't think I can' has the Ken cadence.

jokes.text: MR. ROSSO TRYING TO BREAK INTO HIS CAR WITH A COAT HANGER WHILE SINGING 'IF MY FRIENDS COULD SEE ME NOW' [then]: I LOCKED MY KEYS IN MY MOTHER'S CAR.
jokes.setup: Rosso has been kicked out of his office by the Secret Service and is stuck outside; he's singing to himself about success while stuck in a humiliating predicament
jokes.punchline: He's locked his keys in 'my mother's car' — not even his own car — while singing about how great his friends would think he was if they could see him. The irony is complete.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best sustained visual comedy moment. The layers of irony are exceptional: (1) He's singing 'If My Friends Could See Me Now' about success, (2) while locked out of his mother's car — not even his own, (3) while failing to break in with a coat hanger, (4) having just been ejected from his own office by the Secret Service. The detail that it's his MOTHER'S car is the devastating specific that takes it from good to great. Strong character integration — this is what Rosso's idealism looks like in practice.

jokes.text: THE BUSH PEOPLE FOUND THE ONES YOU WROTE TOO 'SOPHISTICATED.' / SOPHISTICATED? WHAT DOES THAT MEAN? / THAT'S CODE FOR 'NICE TRY, BUT THIS IS A GLORIFIED PHOTO OPPORTUNITY.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay's actual political questions have been rejected by Bush's staff and replaced with 'What is your favorite place to eat in Michigan?'
jokes.punchline: Rosso's translation of 'sophisticated' — 'nice try, but this is a glorified photo opportunity' — is the weary insider knowledge of someone who's been around long enough to recognize PR theater
jokes.explanation: Rosso as the cynical idealist who's had his political hopes ground down by reality is well-used here. 'Glorified photo opportunity' is a good line that still holds up.

jokes.text: OOH. MAYBE IF I'M LUCKY, HE'LL TELL ME WHICH STEAKHOUSE HAS THE BEST PRIME RIB.
jokes.setup: Rosso is trying to put a positive spin on Lindsay having to ask Bush about his favorite restaurant
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's sarcastic riff on the question — imagining Bush's steakhouse recommendations as the pinnacle of political engagement
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's sarcasm is well-placed and in character. 'Best prime rib' is a nicely specific detail for the deflation.

jokes.text: AND NOW ALL MY COMPATRIOTS ARE GETTIN' RICH WORKING WALL STREET, AND I CAN'T GET MY KEYS OUT OF MY MOTHER'S CAR.
jokes.setup: Rosso is lamenting that his Berkeley activism accomplished nothing while his former fellow protesters went on to sell out
jokes.punchline: The contrast between his peers getting rich and him being unable to break into his mother's car is the most devastating possible summary of his life choices
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's great lines. The structural contrast between 'getting rich on Wall Street' and 'can't get my keys out of my mother's car' is perfectly constructed — a whole life's worth of choices compressed into one sentence. The callback to the visual gag pays it off beautifully. This is the kind of joke that makes Freaks and Geeks so beloved — genuinely funny AND genuinely sad. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: SO I TOLD TODD THAT REPUBLICANS AREN'T...SELFISH. IT'S JUST THAT THEY DON'T BELIEVE POOR PEOPLE SHOULD GET HANDOUTS. I MEAN, THEY SHOULD GET JOBS. HANDOUTS JUST MAKE THEM LAZY, DON'T YOU THINK? / UH, I DON'T KNOW. I GUESS SO. / WELL, THEY DO.
jokes.setup: Cindy is talking to Sam; Sam is trying to figure out how to give her a gift
jokes.punchline: Cindy's blithely confident political opinions delivered without any awareness of how they sound; Sam's non-committal 'I guess so' followed by Cindy's firm 'Well, they do.'
jokes.explanation: Another layer of why Sam and Cindy are incompatible. The comedy is Cindy's total certainty combined with Sam's clueless capitulation ('I guess so'). The show threads a needle here between character comedy and actual political observation.

jokes.text: AND SPEAKING OF HANDOUTS, HA HA, UH, I GOT YOU SOMETHING.
jokes.setup: Cindy has just finished lecturing about how poor people shouldn't get handouts from Democrats; Sam is about to give her the heirloom necklace
jokes.punchline: Sam's nervous attempt to pivot from Cindy's political speech to giving her a gift using 'speaking of handouts' as a transition — the pun is weak but his discomfort in the moment is the real joke
jokes.explanation: Sam's nervous laugh ('ha ha') before the pun is what makes this work — his recognition that it's a bad transition but he's committed. The cringe is the comedy.

jokes.text: WHAT IS IT? / IT'S AN HEIRLOOM NECKLACE. IT'S BEEN IN OUR FAMILY FOR GENERATIONS. / HOW MUCH DID IT COST? / UM, I DON'T KNOW. IT'S AN HEIRLOOM.
jokes.setup: Sam gives Cindy the family heirloom necklace with great ceremony
jokes.punchline: Cindy's first response is 'how much did it cost?' — a question that both punctures the romance and reveals her mercenary value system; Sam's flustered 'I don't know, it's an heirloom' is the defeated response
jokes.explanation: Excellent character deflation. The entire weight of the heirloom ceremony (Jean's sentimentality, Sam's nervousness) is destroyed by 'how much did it cost?' The joke also does serious character work — Cindy is revealed as shallow in one line. Sam's helpless repetition of 'it's an heirloom' is the perfect punch.

jokes.text: WELL, DO YOU WANT ME TO PUT IT ON YOU? / NO, THAT'S OK. YOU KNOW, IT'S METAL, SO IT'D BE COLD ON MY NECK AND EVERYTHING.
jokes.setup: Sam offers to put the heirloom necklace on Cindy
jokes.punchline: Cindy refuses with the excuse that metal is cold — possibly the lamest possible excuse for not wearing someone's family heirloom
jokes.explanation: The lameness of 'it's metal, so it'd be cold' as an excuse is perfect — it's just real enough to be a possible excuse but transparently an avoidance. The gap between the sentimental weight of the heirloom and this cold pragmatic excuse is where the comedy lives.

jokes.text: WHAT--IF YOU HAVE A ZIT, YOU'RE NOT ENTITLED TO AN OPINION? IS THAT IT?
jokes.setup: Cindy has told Lindsay she needs to be polite to Bush because he's important and she has pimples
jokes.punchline: Lindsay reframes the class/age-based condescension as skin-based discrimination — 'if you have a zit, you're not entitled to an opinion'
jokes.explanation: A sharp Lindsay line. The logical reduction of the 'be polite to your elders' argument to dermatology is funny and pointed. Good economy.

jokes.text: ISN'T THAT STUPID? THEY'RE AFRAID OF REAL QUESTIONS. / HE'S NOT COMING TO YOUR HIGH SCHOOL TO BE INTERROGATED BY A BUNCH OF PIMPLE-FACED TEENAGERS.
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains about the VP staff vetting her questions; Harold takes a different view
jokes.punchline: Harold's 'pimple-faced teenagers' framing — describing his own children's cohort — is both accurate and hilariously dismissive
jokes.explanation: Harold's pragmatic deflation of teenage political ambitions via 'pimple-faced teenagers' is a good Harold moment. He's not wrong, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: MOM, BUSY DOING WHAT? WAITING FOR THE PRESIDENT TO DIE SO HE CAN TAKE OVER?
jokes.setup: Jean says Bush must be very busy to give him credit for showing up at the school
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's savage summary of the Vice President's actual job description
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's line is genuinely sharp political observation delivered with teenage bluntness. The specific framing ('waiting for the President to die') is dark and accurately describes the VP's position. It's funnier because it's not wrong.

jokes.text: YOUR ONLY AFFILIATION RIGHT NOW TO ANY PARTY IS TO THE WEIR PARTY. WE NEED HELP.
jokes.setup: Harold is trying to get Lindsay to plug his sporting goods store in her question to Bush
jokes.punchline: Harold's pun on 'party' — equating political parties with his family's store — delivered with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: Harold's dad-joke level wordplay is perfectly in character. 'The Weir Party' is a good specific coinage. The delivery — completely earnest, no recognition that this is an absurd thing to say — makes it work.

jokes.text: AND MAYBE YOU COULD WEAR ONE OF THE STORE'S NEW T-SHIRTS. [Harold produces A1 Sporting Goods T-shirt for Lindsay to wear in front of VP Bush]
jokes.setup: Harold is already asking Lindsay to plug his store in her VP question; now he goes further
jokes.punchline: Producing an actual store T-shirt for her to wear in front of the Vice President — the shamelessness of his request escalated to a perfect visual object
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'mention my store' to 'ask his question' to 'wear this T-shirt' is perfectly structured. Harold's complete inability to see the inappropriateness of commodifying his daughter's political moment for sporting goods advertising is peak Harold character comedy.

jokes.text: HE HATES THESE CANS! STAY AWAY FROM THE CANS! [Sam, Cindy, and Bill watching The Jerk in Sam's living room]
jokes.setup: Sam has taken Cindy to see The Jerk as his planned date, wanting to share something he loves
jokes.punchline: Sam and Bill are laughing uproariously at the cans scene; we cut to Cindy watching without laughing — a reaction beat that crystallizes their fundamental incompatibility
jokes.explanation: This scene is beautiful economical storytelling comedy. The contrast between Sam and Bill howling at The Jerk and Cindy's blank non-reaction says everything about why they don't work. The choice of The Jerk — which is about finding belonging and love — as the movie that reveals their incompatibility is subtly perfect. Cindy's non-laugh is the punchline.

jokes.text: WHAT ARE YOU-- OW! WHAT ARE YOU DOING? / I'M GIVING YOU SOMETHING. / OH, GOD, WHAT ARE YOU GIVING ME? / I'M GIVING YOU A HICKEY, YOU GOOF.
jokes.setup: After The Jerk fails to connect them, Cindy makes a move on Sam
jokes.punchline: Sam's confused panic — 'Oh God, what are you giving me?' — in response to a hickey attempt is the perfect expression of his age and inexperience
jokes.explanation: 'Oh God, what are you giving me?' is perfectly Sam — his mind absolutely not going to the obvious conclusion. The 'you goof' from Cindy is an affectionate button that makes her seem more human momentarily.

jokes.text: OH, MY GOD! I'M ENDANGERING YOUR LIFE! COVER ME! / NO, YOU'RE COVERED!
jokes.setup: Bill is watching war movie gunfire; Sam comes in with a hickey and is panicking about their parents seeing it
jokes.punchline: Sam's 'I'M ENDANGERING YOUR LIFE! COVER ME!' is an accidental overlap with what Bill is watching — the movie gunfire and Sam's panic merge briefly into absurdist comedy
jokes.explanation: Functional but not particularly strong. The war movie overlap is a bit of a stretch.

jokes.text: ROSSO'S COOL. DID YOU EVER REALLY LOOK AT HIM? HE'S ACTUALLY KIND OF GOOD-LOOKING. / YEAH, IF YOU'RE ATTRACTED TO GUYS THAT LOOK LIKE JESUS.
jokes.setup: Some of the freaks are talking about Mr. Rosso
jokes.punchline: Kim's qualifier — 'if you're attracted to guys who look like Jesus' — is a great specific burn that contains a compliment within an insult
jokes.explanation: 'Guys that look like Jesus' is a perfectly specific visual description and a funny framing. It's complimentary of a specific aesthetic while being withering about it. Kim's dry delivery is perfect for this.

jokes.text: KEN PUNCHES DANIEL IN THE FACE [sudden, unexplained physical outburst]
jokes.setup: The freaks are having a casual hangout; Ken is going through internal turmoil about Amy
jokes.punchline: Ken suddenly punches Daniel for no apparent reason — the eruption of his emotional turmoil into random violence is played for dark comedy
jokes.explanation: The sudden punch lands because it's so unexpected in tone — the scene has been relaxed, and then Ken just hits Daniel. The dark comedy of someone processing emotional confusion through random violence is characteristic of the show's willingness to be genuinely strange.

jokes.text: I'M GONNA BREAK UP WITH CINDY SANDERS. / WHY? / SHE'S HOT. [Ken's bathroom interlude with Sam]
jokes.setup: Sam reveals he's going to break up with Cindy; a guy (Ken) in the bathroom asks why
jokes.punchline: The response 'she's hot' as a counter-argument to breaking up — the simplest possible objection delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: Good economy. 'She's hot' as a complete counter-argument is perfectly teenage and deadpan.

jokes.text: YEAH, I THINK I'M GONNA GET A 'B' IN MATH. I'M SO RELIEVED. / [Sam awkwardly transitions to asking her on a proper date]
jokes.setup: Sam approaches Cindy to ask her on a real date
jokes.punchline: Cindy's opener — 'I think I'm gonna get a B in math, I'm so relieved' — is the most aggressively non-interesting thing she could say, setting up the incompatibility theme
jokes.explanation: A subtle character moment — Cindy's conversation opener is maximally boring. It's not a big joke but it reinforces the episode's throughline about their incompatibility.

jokes.text: YEARS FROM NOW YOU'RE GONNA BE SITTING IN YOUR HOUSE, LOOKING AT YOUR UNATTRACTIVE KIDS WITH YOUR UNATTRACTIVE WIFE, SAYING TO YOURSELF, 'MAN, WHY DID I EVER DUMP THAT GODDESS CINDY SANDERS?'
jokes.setup: Neal is arguing against Sam breaking up with Cindy
jokes.punchline: Neal's prediction of Sam's future — with its cheerful specificity of 'unattractive kids with your unattractive wife' — is simultaneously crushing and hilarious; it's the most brutal possible argument for staying with someone you don't like
jokes.explanation: This is excellent Neal character comedy. His romantic fatalism — the idea that this is the best Sam will ever do and he's throwing it away — is both darkly funny and painfully revealing of Neal's own insecurities. 'Unattractive kids with your unattractive wife' is delivered as prophecy, not insult, which makes it funnier. Neal's grandiose despair is the character in perfect form.

jokes.text: HAVE I EVER TOLD YOU ABOUT THE TIME THAT I MADE OUT WITH VICKI APPLEBY? / OH, SHUT UP. YOU'RE SUCH A LIAR.
jokes.setup: Sam has shut down Neal's advice about Cindy; Neal pivots to his favorite topic — his own romantic history
jokes.punchline: Bill's instant dismissal 'you're such a liar' — the implication that Neal tells this story constantly and nobody believes it
jokes.explanation: A good Neal character beat. The implication that this is an oft-repeated and never-believed story is efficiently communicated. Bill's flat 'you're such a liar' is perfectly dry.

jokes.text: IT'S JUST WE DON'T HAVE ANYTHING IN COMMON. I MEAN, SHE THOUGHT THE JERK WAS STUPID. / YOU SERIOUS? MAN, MY GIRLFRIEND LOVED THE JERK. [bathroom conversation between Sam and Ken]
jokes.setup: Sam is explaining his incompatibility with Cindy; Ken is about to reveal his own girlfriend trouble
jokes.punchline: Ken's revelation that his girlfriend loves The Jerk — the thing Sam wishes Cindy did — makes Ken sound like he has the perfect relationship, which makes his subsequent 'I have to break up with her too' even more ironic
jokes.explanation: This exchange is structurally elegant — Sam envies what Ken has (girlfriend who likes The Jerk) while Ken is wrestling with breaking up with her for entirely different reasons. The irony is rich without being belabored.

jokes.text: SAM, YOU'RE WEARING A TURTLENECK. / ARE YOU COVERING UP MY HICKEY? / WHAT? NO! NO, I-- / IT'S KIND OF EMBARRASSING.
jokes.setup: Sam has been wearing a turtleneck to hide the hickey Cindy gave him
jokes.punchline: Cindy calls him out immediately, and Sam's flustered denial is undone by the turtleneck itself; then Cindy says it's 'kind of embarrassing' that he'd hide evidence of her affection
jokes.explanation: There's a nice ironic parallel being established — Cindy won't wear Sam's gift and Sam won't display Cindy's. But Cindy's obliviousness to this hypocrisy (see joke 39) is where the comedy lives.

jokes.text: IT'S KIND OF EMBARRASSING. / COME ON, DON'T GET ALL MAD. / OH, THANKS, SAM. SO I GUESS IF YOU GOT ME A DIAMOND RING AND I DIDN'T WEAR IT BECAUSE I WAS TOO EMBARRASSED, YOU WOULDN'T GET ALL MAD, EITHER?
jokes.setup: Cindy confronts Sam about the turtleneck covering her hickey
jokes.punchline: Cindy's hypothetical 'diamond ring' counter-argument — which inadvertently highlights that they've been together about 12 minutes
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the scale mismatch — Cindy's escalation to diamond rings in a relationship where the main activities have been mall visits. Good cringe material in the run-up to the breakup.

jokes.text: COME ON! I GOT YOU A NECKLACE, AND YOU'VE NEVER WORN IT. / IT WAS UGLY!
jokes.setup: Sam calls out Cindy's hypocrisy about his hidden hickey by pointing out she never wore his necklace
jokes.punchline: Cindy's instant 'IT WAS UGLY!' — no pretense, no apology, just direct brutal honesty about the heirloom his grandmother gave his mother
jokes.explanation: This is a great joke. The entire episode has been building the heirloom necklace as a sincere symbol of Sam's love — his mother's sentimentality, his grandmother's legacy. Cindy's 'IT WAS UGLY!' delivers the most brutal possible verdict in two words with no modulation whatsoever. The economy is nearly perfect. The callback payoff is well-earned. Only Cindy — self-absorbed, tactless, not meaning to be cruel — could say this so cleanly.

jokes.text: SO I GUESS IF YOU GOT ME A DIAMOND RING AND I DIDN'T WEAR IT BECAUSE I WAS TOO EMBARRASSED, YOU WOULDN'T GET ALL MAD, EITHER? / HEY, COME ON! I GOT YOU A NECKLACE, AND YOU'VE NEVER WORN IT. / IT WAS UGLY!
jokes.setup: Cindy and Sam are fighting about the turtleneck/hickey; it escalates to the necklace
jokes.punchline: Cindy's counter — deploying the diamond ring hypothetical — is undercut immediately by Sam pointing out the necklace; her response 'IT WAS UGLY' is the punchline. The heirloom that Jean described as elegant, that Sam gave with great tenderness, is simply ugly.
jokes.explanation: IT WAS UGLY is a perfect punchline to the necklace through-line. Jean's grandmother's heirloom is ugly. Cindy has thought so the entire time. The episode has built this through multiple scenes — Jean's reverence, Sam's hope, Cindy's cold-neck excuse — and the payoff is one brutal word. Earned callback with high impact. The structure rewards careful viewing.

jokes.text: YOU CAN'T BREAK UP WITH ME. YOU'RE SUPPOSED TO BE NICE. THAT'S THE ONLY REASON WHY I'M GOING OUT WITH YOU IN THE FIRST PLACE.
jokes.setup: Sam breaks up with Cindy; Cindy says he can't
jokes.punchline: Cindy's logic for why Sam can't break up with her — 'you're supposed to be nice' — and her revelation that she only went out with him because he was nice (not because she liked him)
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's darkest and most perfectly constructed character joke. Cindy's logic is simultaneously hilarious and devastating: (1) breaking up is not 'nice,' therefore Sam's niceness prohibits him from doing it, (2) she only dated him because he was nice — not because she liked him. The joke perfectly demolishes the premise of Sam's entire romantic pursuit. It's the kind of writing that makes an audience laugh and wince simultaneously. Character integration is exceptional — only Cindy, who has never examined her own shallowness, could deliver this with complete sincerity.

jokes.text: HEY, I AM NICE! I'M JUST NOT HAVING ANY FUN. ARE YOU? / NO. NO, I'M NOT HAVING ANY FUN, SAM.
jokes.setup: Sam challenges Cindy's assessment of him
jokes.punchline: The moment they both admit they're not having fun — Cindy's honest 'No, I'm not having any fun, Sam' delivered apparently without irony, as if she'd never thought to question this
jokes.explanation: The genuine, mutual admission that the relationship has been no fun is both sad and funny. Cindy's delivery of 'No, I'm not having any fun, Sam' as a revelation she's having in real time is perfectly character-specific.

jokes.text: HAVE YOUR STUPID HEIRLOOM BACK. [Cindy throws the necklace at Sam]
jokes.setup: The breakup scene has gone badly; Cindy is furious
jokes.punchline: Cindy produces the necklace she said she'd never wear because it was cold/ugly and throws it back — revealing she had it all along
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the heirloom necklace running joke. She had it. She just wasn't wearing it. 'Your STUPID heirloom' — the descriptor is the funny part, reducing three generations of family sentiment to 'stupid.' Good physical button to the whole arc.

jokes.text: UM, YOU DID THE RIGHT THING. YOU'RE TOO GOOD FOR HER. ARE YOU GONNA EAT WITH US AT LUNCH TODAY? / YEAH, YEAH. / THANK GOD.
jokes.setup: The breakup is over; Sam's friends immediately respond
jokes.punchline: Bill's 'THANK GOD' about Sam eating lunch with them again — the revelation that Sam's romance was primarily inconvenient for Bill's lunch arrangements
jokes.explanation: Bill's 'Thank God' is one of the episode's most perfectly economical moments. The quick transition from 'you're too good for her' (genuine support) to 'are you gonna eat with us at lunch' (selfish concern) to 'THANK GOD' (pure relief) is three beats that tell you everything about Bill. He means the first part sincerely — and also really missed having his friend at lunch.

jokes.text: SIR, ARE YOU NOW OR HAVE YOU EVER BEEN A MEMBER OF AN ORGANIZATION CALLED THE TAFT STUDENTS ALLIANCE FOR A NEW AMERICA? / I MEAN, I MIGHT BE ON THEIR MAILING LIST SOMEWHERE FROM WAY BACK...
jokes.setup: Rosso is trying to attend the Bush assembly in his own school; the Secret Service has red-flagged him
jokes.punchline: Rosso's answer to whether he's a member of a radical organization — 'I might be on their mailing list somewhere from way back' — is the most damning possible defense
jokes.explanation: Rosso's 'I might be on their mailing list' is a perfect self-incrimination. He's trying to say no but can't quite commit to the denial — and 'from way back' is the exact evasion of someone who genuinely doesn't remember all the radical organizations he signed up for at Berkeley. Perfect character consistency.

jokes.text: SEE, I TOLD YOU THAT GUY WAS A KOOK. [Harold, smugly, as Rosso is escorted away by Secret Service]
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso is being removed from his own school by Secret Service
jokes.punchline: Harold's completely self-satisfied vindication — he always thought Rosso was suspicious, and now the government agrees
jokes.explanation: Harold's smug 'kook' verdict is good character comedy. His conservative worldview getting accidental validation from the Secret Service is a nice irony.

jokes.text: SOMETIMES I HAVE TO JUST STAND IN ONE PLACE FOR 12 HOURS AND STARE AT A WALL. YOU START TO SEE THINGS. YOUR FEET-- THE PAIN.
jokes.setup: Rosso is being held in a 'holding area' by Secret Service; he describes his job to the agent
jokes.punchline: Rosso describing guidance counseling as staring at a wall for 12 hours until you 'start to see things' — the existential horror of his job delivered conversationally
jokes.explanation: A gem of a Rosso moment. His description of guidance counseling — standing in one place, staring at a wall, seeing things, foot pain — is a darkly funny portrait of a man slowly losing his mind in a thankless job. 'You start to see things' is the line. The agent's 'Well, it's an important job' / Rosso's 'No, it's not' button is perfect.

jokes.text: SOMETIMES I HAVE TO JUST STAND IN ONE PLACE FOR 12 HOURS AND STARE AT A WALL. YOU START TO SEE THINGS. YOUR FEET-- THE PAIN.
jokes.setup: Rosso is stuck in his 'holding area' with a Secret Service agent; he's trying to explain what being a guidance counselor is like
jokes.punchline: Rosso's description of counseling — standing and staring at walls for 12 hours until he hallucates — is delivered with complete earnestness as if this is a normal job requirement
jokes.explanation: Rosso finding some strange profundity in the miserable parts of his job is very in character. 'You start to see things' followed by 'your feet -- the pain' is a good comedic non-sequitur that undercuts any philosophical pretension.

jokes.text: WELL, IT'S AN IMPORTANT JOB. / YOU EVER HEARD OF THE VICE PRESIDENT GETTING ASSASSINATED? / NO. YOU KNOW WHY? IT'S NEVER HAPPENED. WILL IT EVER HAPPEN? NO WAY. BECAUSE WHO CARES? KNOW WHAT I MEAN?
jokes.setup: The Secret Service agent politely says Rosso's counseling job is important; Rosso responds by pointing out the irrelevance of protecting the Vice President
jokes.punchline: Rosso's darkly logical argument — no VP has ever been assassinated because no one cares enough about the VP to bother — is a genuinely subversive piece of reasoning delivered to the man literally there to protect the VP
jokes.explanation: This is one of Rosso's best moments in the episode. The logic — no VP has ever been assassinated because 'who cares?' — is both historically accurate and darkly funny. Delivering this to a Secret Service agent whose entire job is protecting the VP is a perfect ironic audience. 'WILL IT EVER HAPPEN? NO WAY. BECAUSE WHO CARES?' is a legitimately great comedic proposition. Subversive, well-constructed, and only Rosso would say it.

jokes.text: YEAH, HAIL TO THE CHIEF! / THIS SONG ROCKS!
jokes.setup: The school band plays 'Hail to the Chief' as Bush enters
jokes.punchline: Someone in the audience shouts 'THIS SONG ROCKS!' — the delightful obliviousness of a high schooler getting genuinely excited about the most stately and un-rock piece of music possible
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist beat. 'THIS SONG ROCKS' about 'Hail to the Chief' is funny because it's so wrong in such a specific way. Also a nice callback button to Amy's anxiety about the performance.

jokes.text: YOU KNOW, SOMETIMES--I MEAN, THIS SOUNDS CRAZY--BUT SOMETIMES I THINK I JUST WANT TO RIP OFF MY VEST AND JACKET AND JUST PUT ON A T-SHIRT AND GO AND, LIKE, MAKE PANCAKES SOMEWHERE OR SOMETHING. BUT, I MEAN, THAT WOULD BE CRAZY, RIGHT?
jokes.setup: The Vice President of the United States is at a high school assembly; a guidance counselor is stuck with him
jokes.punchline: Bush has a moment of raw humanization — expressing his desire to just make pancakes — delivered to a guidance counselor who has a career aptitude test at the ready
jokes.explanation: A surprisingly good comedic beat. Making the VP a person who fantasizes about pancakes while doing his job is humanizing and funny. The 'rip off my vest' specificity is good. Paired with Rosso's aptitude test offer, it creates a gentle comedy of mutual displacement.

jokes.text: SOMETIMES-- I MEAN, THIS SOUNDS CRAZY-- BUT SOMETIMES I THINK I JUST WANT TO RIP OFF MY VEST AND JACKET AND JUST PUT ON A T-SHIRT AND GO AND, LIKE, MAKE PANCAKES SOMEWHERE OR SOMETHING. BUT, I MEAN, THAT WOULD BE CRAZY, RIGHT?
jokes.setup: Bush is surrounded by full ceremonial security while students watch from the cafeteria
jokes.punchline: Bush's candid, relatable confession that he just wants to ditch the formality and make pancakes — the most powerful political figure in the room having a mundane human crisis
jokes.explanation: A nicely humanizing/absurdist touch — the VP having a moment of wanting to be a normal person ('make pancakes somewhere or something') in the middle of the full security circus. 'But that would be crazy, right?' is a good button. The mundane specificity of pancakes elevates it.

jokes.text: HEY, I'VE GOT A TEST WHICH REVEALS WHAT TYPE OF JOB YOU'RE BEST SUITED FOR. YOU WANT TO TAKE IT?
jokes.setup: VP Bush has just confided his desire to escape his prestigious job and make pancakes
jokes.punchline: Rosso immediately pivots to offering him a career aptitude test — treating the Vice President exactly like a confused high schooler
jokes.explanation: Rosso offering the VP a career aptitude test is a perfect comedic moment. The joke works because: (1) Rosso has one tool and applies it to everyone, (2) Bush has literally just expressed career dissatisfaction, (3) the incongruity of treating the second most powerful man in America as a lost teenager is surreally appropriate, and (4) Rosso is completely sincere. This is character comedy at its most elegant.

jokes.text: MR. VICE PRESIDENT, MY NAME IS LINDSAY WEIR. MY DAD OWNS A1 SPORTING GOODS ON 16 MILE ROAD. MY QUESTION IS... WHY DID YOUR STAFF REJECT MY QUESTION? ARE YOU AFRAID OF AN OPEN DISCOURSE WITH THE STUDENTS?
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been told to ask Bush a safe question about his favorite Michigan restaurant; she has been pressured by her father to mention his sporting goods store
jokes.punchline: Lindsay compromises by mentioning the store exactly as Harold asked — and then completely ignores the safe question to challenge Bush directly. She delivers both parental obligations and personal integrity simultaneously, in the most awkward possible combination.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's crowning comedic moment and deserves its score. The writing is structurally exceptional: Lindsay has been pressured by (1) Bush's staff to ask a safe question, (2) her father to mention the store, (3) Rosso to represent the school well. Her solution is to obey Harold's request ('My dad owns A1 Sporting Goods') and then immediately pivot to defiance ('Why did your staff reject my question?'). It pays off THREE separate setups simultaneously, is completely in character (Lindsay's principled stubbornness, her love of her dad), generates a perfect audience reaction, and is genuinely surprising despite being inevitable in retrospect. The absurdist juxtaposition of small-business advertising with political courage is perfect. 'FUNNY KID. ONE OF McKINLEY'S FINEST.' from Bush is the ideal button.

jokes.text: FUNNY KID. ONE OF McKINLEY'S FINEST. / 'DO YOU LIKE WORKING WITH MAJOR APPLIANCES?' / THAT'D BE A YES.
jokes.setup: Bush has just been publicly challenged by Lindsay; Rosso is still in his holding area with Bush
jokes.punchline: Bush deflects Lindsay's challenge with political charm; then Rosso reads another rejected question ('Do you like working with major appliances?') and Bush's 'That'd be a yes' — accepting even this absurd question with a politician's vague affirmation
jokes.explanation: The button on the episode's main plot is well-constructed. 'THAT'D BE A YES' as Bush's answer to whether he likes major appliances — delivered with the reflexive positivity of a career politician — is perfectly judged. Rosso reading the rejected questions to Bush in private is a great ironic coda.

tmdb_overview: Lindsay tries to convince Harold to let her join her friends at a Who concert. Kim and Lindsay accidentally run over Millie's dog. Kim starts hanging out with Millie. Lindsay wants to tell Millie the truth about her dog, but Kim doesn't. Meanwhile, Nick teaches himself to play guitar and writes a love ballad for Lindsay and decides to play it for her despite Kens' objections. Ms. Haverchuck stuns Bill with the news that she has been dating Coach Fredricks.
